meta.scene_summary: We are busy building the future with AI assistants we don't trust, while the past keeps leaking our secrets.

cooking.headline: Alibaba thinks your AI coding assistant is a double agent
cooking.summary: Alibaba is banning Claude Code over concerns that the AI might be leaking company secrets back to its creators. It's the corporate equivalent of firing your intern because you're worried they're telling your competitors how you make the coffee.
cooking.plain_english: Alibaba is restricting the use of Claude Code in its offices due to fears that the software could create unauthorized access points to company data.

cooking.headline: Virginia just made your phone's location history a lot less valuable to data brokers
cooking.summary: Virginia is banning the sale of precise geolocation data, effectively telling data brokers that your late-night Taco Bell runs are now off-limits for profit. It turns out even your smartphone needs a privacy policy that doesn't treat your physical location like a public commodity.
cooking.plain_english: Virginia has passed a law that prohibits the sale of exact location data collected from mobile devices.

cooking.headline: The US government is now the ultimate bag holder
cooking.summary: The federal government has officially diversified its portfolio by becoming a shareholder in 26 different companies. It's comforting to know that when our code breaks in production, the government might actually be one of the people getting paged.
cooking.plain_english: The United States government currently holds equity stakes in 26 private corporations.

cooking.headline: Java is finally forcing you to actually initialize your fields
cooking.summary: The JVM is moving toward stricter field initialization, essentially acting like that one professor who refuses to accept homework with missing headers. It turns out even programming languages have reached the point where they've lost patience with your 'I'll fix the null pointer later' attitude.
cooking.plain_english: Java is introducing a new feature that requires developers to explicitly assign values to object fields, reducing the likelihood of errors caused by uninitialized data.

cooking.headline: LMDB hits 1.0, proving that sometimes the best software is the kind that just stays quiet
cooking.summary: After years of being the silent engine powering everything important, LMDB has finally hit version 1.0. It's like that one coworker who does all the heavy lifting in the background and only speaks up once every decade to tell you the job is done.
cooking.plain_english: The Lightning Memory-Mapped Database (LMDB) has reached its official 1.0 release, marking a stable milestone for the high-performance database library.

cooked.headline: The FBI just sent the proxy market to detention
cooked.summary: The FBI seized NetNut and the Popa botnet, effectively pulling the plug on a massive infrastructure used to mask internet traffic. It turns out that when you build a business model on being invisible, the feds eventually find a way to make you the center of attention.
cooked.plain_english: The FBI shut down a proxy service and a network of compromised computers that were being used to hide malicious online activity.

cooked.headline: The Intercept's secure tip line was basically an unattended group chat for months
cooked.summary: The Intercept managed to lose control of their Signal-based tip line, turning a tool meant for high-stakes whistleblowing into a ghost town where nobody had the keys. It is the ultimate security irony: the 'secure' channel was so secure that even the people running it couldn't get in.
cooked.plain_english: The Intercept failed to manage its secure communication channel for a period of several months, meaning they were unable to access incoming tips.

cooked.headline: Linux decided your encryption keys deserve a permanent vacation in RAM
cooked.summary: Since version 6.9, suspending your laptop no longer wipes LUKS encryption keys from memory, effectively leaving your data's front door wide open. It's the digital equivalent of locking your house but leaving the spare key taped to the doormat for anyone who wanders by.
cooked.plain_english: A recent update to the Linux operating system prevents the automatic deletion of disk encryption keys when a computer enters sleep mode, making it easier for unauthorized users to access data on a suspended device.

cooked.headline: Zuckerberg realizes that firing everyone might not be a business strategy
cooked.summary: Meta spent years trimming the team to 'efficiency' only for Zuckerberg to realize that losing all your institutional knowledge actually makes the company run worse. It turns out that firing the people who know how things work is a great way to discover that things stop working.
cooked.plain_english: Mark Zuckerberg has acknowledged that the massive layoffs implemented at Meta have negatively impacted the company's productivity and operational effectiveness.

cooked.headline: Zuckerberg admits AI agents are taking longer than his haircut
cooked.summary: Meta promised us autonomous agents that would handle our lives, but apparently, they're still struggling with basic object permanence. It turns out that teaching a model to do your chores is just as painful as managing an intern who needs a manual for everything.
cooked.plain_english: Meta has announced that the development of its AI agent software is behind its original schedule.
